subject: Chemistry
q:
Ka1,Ka2 and Ka3 are the respective ionization constants for the following reactions a,b and c.

(a) H2C2O4⇌H++HC2O4-

(b) HC2O4-⇌H++HC2O42-

(c) H2C2O4⇌2H++C2O42-

The relationship between Ka1, Ka2 and Ka3 is given as
question:
Ka1,Ka2 and Ka3 are the respective ionization constants for the following reactions a,b and c.

(a) H2C2O4⇌H++HC2O4-

(b) HC2O4-⇌H++HC2O42-

(c) H2C2O4⇌2H++C2O42-

The relationship between Ka1, Ka2 and Ka3 is given as
solution:
H2C2O4⇌H++HC2O4-Ka1

H2C2O4-⇌H++C2O42-Ka2

H2C2O4⇌2H++C2O42-   Ka3

On adding equation 1 and equation 2 we get equation 3. So, Ka3=Ka1×Ka2.

(A) 0.01 MHCl
(B) 0.01 MNaOH

(D) 0.01 MNaCl

solution:

pH of 0.01MNaOH=12
pH of 0.01MCH3COONa>7
pH of 0.01MNaCl=7
So the order of pH:B>C>D>A

So the order of pOH:  B<C<D<A

subject: Chemistry
q: In an acid-base titration, 0.1 M HCl solution was added to the NaOH solution of unknown strength. Which of the following correctly shown the change of pH of the titration mixture in this experiment?
question: In an acid-base titration, 0.1 M HCl solution was added to the NaOH solution of unknown strength. Which of the following correctly shown the change of pH of the titration mixture in this experiment?
solution:
For titration of strong base with strong acid pH changes sharply near equivalence point and at equivalent point pH sharply decreases because strong base & strong acid dissociate completely.
HClSA + NaOHSB→NaCl+ H2Ol
at equivalent point pH=7 and at endpoint solution will be neutral.

subject: Chemistry
q:
What would be the $\mathrm{pH}$ of a solution obtained by mixing $5 \mathrm{~g}$ of acetic acid and $7.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of sodium acetate and making the volume equal to $500 \mathrm{~mL}$ ? 
$\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=1.75 \times 10^{-5}, \mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}=4.76\right)$
question:
What would be the $\mathrm{pH}$ of a solution obtained by mixing $5 \mathrm{~g}$ of acetic acid and $7.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of sodium acetate and making the volume equal to $500 \mathrm{~mL}$ ? 
$\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=1.75 \times 10^{-5}, \mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}=4.76\right)$
options: $\mathrm{pH}$ of solution will be equal to $\mathrm{pH}$ of acetic acid
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{pH} & =\mathrm{pK}_a+\log \frac{[\text { salt }]}{[\text { acid }]} \\
& =4.76+\log \frac{\frac{7.5}{500}}{\frac{5}{500}}=4.7+\log 1.5=4.87
\end{aligned}$
Hence correct answer is $4.76 < \mathrm{pH} < 5.0$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The equilibrium constant for decomposition of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g})\left(\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=92.34 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
is $8.0 \times 10^{-3}$ at 2300 K and total pressure at equilibrium is 1 bar. Under this condition, the degree of dissociation $(\alpha)$ of water is ________ $\times 10^{-2}$ (nearest integer value).
[Assume $\alpha$ is negligible with respect to 1 ]
question:
The equilibrium constant for decomposition of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g})\left(\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=92.34 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
is $8.0 \times 10^{-3}$ at 2300 K and total pressure at equilibrium is 1 bar. Under this condition, the degree of dissociation $(\alpha)$ of water is ________ $\times 10^{-2}$ (nearest integer value).
[Assume $\alpha$ is negligible with respect to 1 ]
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \\ & \mathrm{t}=0 \quad 1 \mathrm{~mole} \\ & \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{eq}} \quad 1-\alpha \quad \alpha \quad \frac{\alpha}{2} \\ & \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{T}}=1+\frac{\alpha}{2} \simeq 1(\alpha \ll 1) \\ & \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{H}_2} \cdot \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_2}^{1 / 2}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}}=\frac{(\alpha \cdot \mathrm{P})\left(\frac{\alpha}{2} \mathrm{P}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{(1-\alpha) \mathrm{P}} \\ & 8 \times 10^{-3}=\frac{\alpha^{3 / 2}}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \alpha^{3 / 2}=8 \sqrt{2} \times 10^{-3} \\ & \alpha^3=128 \times 10^{-6} \\ & \alpha=\sqrt[3]{128} \times 10^{-2} \\ & =5.03 \times 10^{-2}\end{aligned}$

When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi><mo> </mo><mi>of</mi><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COONa</mi></math> solution is mixed with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi><mo> </mo><mi>of</mi><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi></math> solution, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> of the resultant solution is found to be equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is _______.
question: The dissociation constant of acetic is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo></math>. When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi><mo> </mo><mi>of</mi><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COONa</mi></math> solution is mixed with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi><mo> </mo><mi>of</mi><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi></math> solution, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> of the resultant solution is found to be equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is _______.
solution:
The Henderson equation for the given acidic buffer can be written as 

pH=pKa+logsaltweak acid

5=pKa+log25×0.225×0.02⇒pKa=4⇒Ka=10-4=10×10-5

Hence, the value of x is 10.

When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi><mo> </mo><mi>of</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> is added, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>BaSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> just begins to precipitate. What is the original concentration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Ba</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math>?
solution:
Ba+2+SO42−⇌BaSO4(s)ppt

Final conc. of [SO42−]=MV1V1+V2=1×50500=0.1M

Final conc.of [Ba+2] When BaSO4 start precipitating

KSP=QSP=[Ba+2][SO42−]

10−10=[Ba+2](0.1M)

Initial conc. [Ba+2]; initial volume was =500-50=450 ml
M1V1=M2V2

M1=M2V2V1=10−9×500450

M1=1.1×10−9M

subject: Chemistry
q:
The titration curve of weak acid vs. strong base with phenolphthalein as indicator is shown below. The Kphenolphthalein =4×10-10

Given: log 2 = 0.3

The number of following statement/s which is/are correct about phenolphthalein is _______

A. It can be used as an indicator for the titration of weak acid with weak base.

B. It begins to change colour at pH = 8.4

C. It is a weak organic base

D. It is colourless in acidic medium
question:
The titration curve of weak acid vs. strong base with phenolphthalein as indicator is shown below. The Kphenolphthalein =4×10-10

Given: log 2 = 0.3

The number of following statement/s which is/are correct about phenolphthalein is _______

A. It can be used as an indicator for the titration of weak acid with weak base.

B. It begins to change colour at pH = 8.4

C. It is a weak organic base

D. It is colourless in acidic medium
solution:
Phenolphthalein is an organic acid and can be represented as HPh.

HPh (colourless) ⇌H++Ph- (Pink) 

Using Henderson equation for phenolphthalein

pH=pKIn+logPh-[HPh]

At equivalence point  Ph-=[HPh]

∴pH2=pKIn=-log[4]+10=-0.6+10=9.4

Hence at (9.4±1)PH , phenolphthalein starts changing colour. Phenolphthalein is colourless in acidic medium and pink in basic medium. Phenolphthalein indicator distinguish the pH change between 8 to 10. Therefore, it is used for strong acid and strong base titration or weak acid and strong base titration. Hence, A and C are incorrect statements.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?

(A) The pH of 1×10–8 M HCl solution is 8.

(B) The conjugate base of H2PO4- is HPO42-.

(C) Kw increases with increase in temperature.

(D) When a solution of a weak monoprotic acid is titrated against a strong base at half neutralisation point, pH=12pKa. Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?

(A) The pH of 1×10–8 M HCl solution is 8.

(B) The conjugate base of H2PO4- is HPO42-.

(C) Kw increases with increase in temperature.

(D) When a solution of a weak monoprotic acid is titrated against a strong base at half neutralisation point, pH=12pKa. Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
When concentration of H+ ion is more than 10-7M, the proton concentration from water must be considered. Hence, the pH of 10-8M HCl solution is not equal to 8.

H2PO4-Acid⇌HPO42-Conjugate base+H+

Kw increase with increase in temperature as dissociation of water increases with increase in temperature.

When weak monoprotic acid is titrated against strong base, at half neutralization point, the solution becomes acidic buffer and its pH is given by

pH=pKa+logSaltAcid

⇒pH=pKa+0 as salt=acid

q:
The solubility product of PbI2 is 8.0×10-9. The solubility of lead iodide in 0.1 molar solution of lead nitrate is x×10-6 mol/L. The value of x is _________ (Rounded off to the nearest integer)

[Given 2=1.41]
question:
The solubility product of PbI2 is 8.0×10-9. The solubility of lead iodide in 0.1 molar solution of lead nitrate is x×10-6 mol/L. The value of x is _________ (Rounded off to the nearest integer)

[Given 2=1.41]
solution:
Given: KspPbI2=8×10-9

To calculate : solubility of PbI2 in 0.1M solution of PbNO22

I PbNO32→Pb(aq)+2+2NO3-aq

0.1 M---         0.1 M         0.2 M

II PbI2s⇌Pb+2aq+2I-aq

                         s                2s

[Pb+2]=s+0.1

≃0.1

Now: Ksp=8×10-9=Pb+2I-2

⇒8×10-9=0.1×2 s2

⇒8×10-8=4 s2⇒s=2×10-4

⇒S=141×10-6M

⇒x=141

subject: Chemistry
q:
The solubility product of BaSO4 is 1×10-10 at 298 K. The solubility of BaSO4 in 0.1M K2SO4(aq) solution is    ------×10-9 g L-1(nearest integer).
Given: Molar mass of BaSO4 is 233 g mol-1
question:
The solubility product of BaSO4 is 1×10-10 at 298 K. The solubility of BaSO4 in 0.1M K2SO4(aq) solution is    ------×10-9 g L-1(nearest integer).
Given: Molar mass of BaSO4 is 233 g mol-1
solution:
For the insoluble salt barium sulphate, sulphate ion added is the common ion. Due to the common ion effect the solubility of barium sulphate decreases. Let us assume solubility of barium sulphate is S.

BaSO4s⇌Ba2+Saq+SO42-S+0.1aq
 K2SO40.1-0.1⟶ K+0.1aq+SO42-0.1aq
KSP=Ba2+SO42-
1×10-10=S(S+0.1)
≃0.1 S
S=10-9M=233×10-9gL-1

subject: Chemistry
q: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>310</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, the solubility of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CaF</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>34</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math>. The solubility product of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CaF</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is ---- <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>mol</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>3</mn></msup></math>(nearest integer). (Given molar mass : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CaF</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>78</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>310</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, the solubility of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CaF</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>34</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math>. The solubility product of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CaF</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is ---- <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>mol</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>3</mn></msup></math>(nearest integer). (Given molar mass : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CaF</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>78</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
The solubility product constant is the equilibrium constant for the dissolution of a solid substance into an aqueous solution. It is denoted by the symbol Ksp.

Solubility of CaF2=S mole//L

S=2.34×10-30.1×78=2.3478×10-2=3×10-4 mol/L

KspCaF2=4 S3=43×10-43

=108×10-12

=0.0108×10-8mol/L3

subject: Chemistry
q: When rain is accompanied by a thunderstorm, the collected rain water will have a $\mathrm{pH}$ value
question: When rain is accompanied by a thunderstorm, the collected rain water will have a $\mathrm{pH}$ value
options: slightly higher than that when the thunderstorm is not there
which depends on the amount of dust in air
slightly lower than that of rain water without thunderstorm
solution: The rain water after thunderstorm contains dissolved acid and therefore the $\mathrm{pH}$ is less than rain water without thunderstorm.

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></msup></math> value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>001</mn><mi>MNaOH</mi></math> solution is
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></msup></math> value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>001</mn><mi>MNaOH</mi></math> solution is
solution:
Concentration of NaOH given is 0.001M

OH-=0.001=1×10-3 

pOH=-logOH-

=-log1×10-3=

=3 pH=14-pOH

=14-3=11

Hence, the pH of the given solution of NaOH is 11.

(Integer answer)
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is a sparingly soluble salt of molar mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> and solubility <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The solubility product satisfies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>sp</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mn>5</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> is __________ . (Integer answer)
solution:
Solubility =xm mole/lit
A3B2s⇌3A2+3xM(aqs)+2B3-(aqs)2xM
Ksp=3xM32xM2
=3322xm5
=27×4xm5
=108xm5=axM5
So a=108

q:
An aqueous solution of HCl with pH 1.0 is diluted by adding equal volume of water (ignoring dissociation of water). The pH of HCl solution would
(Given $\log 2=0.30)$
question:
An aqueous solution of HCl with pH 1.0 is diluted by adding equal volume of water (ignoring dissociation of water). The pH of HCl solution would
(Given $\log 2=0.30)$
solution:
$\mathrm{HCl}_{(\mathrm{aq})} \mathrm{pH}=1 ;\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-1}$
If equal volume of water is added concentration will become half
$\begin{aligned}& {\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]_{\mathrm{sol}}=\frac{10^{-1}}{2}} \\& \mathrm{pH}=1.3\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: The first and second dissociation constants of an acid $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{A}$ are $1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ and $5.0 \times 10^{-10}$ respectively. The overall dissociation constant of the acid will be
question: The first and second dissociation constants of an acid $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{A}$ are $1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ and $5.0 \times 10^{-10}$ respectively. The overall dissociation constant of the acid will be
solution:
$\begin{array}{ll}\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{HA}^{-}+\mathrm{H}^{+} & \mathrm{K}_1=\frac{\left[\mathrm{HA}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{A}\right]}  \quad \dots (1)\\ \mathrm{HA}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{A}^{2-} & \mathrm{K}_2=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{A}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{HA}^{-}\right]}\quad \dots(2)\end{array}$
For the reaction
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{A} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{A}^{2-} \\
& \mathrm{K}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^2\left[\mathrm{~A}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{A}\right]}=\mathrm{K}_1 \times \mathrm{K}_2 \\
& =1 \times 10^{-5} \times 5 \times 10^{-10} \\
& =5 \times 10^{-15}
\end{aligned}$
Hence, (C) is correct.

q:
Consider the following statements
(a) The pH of a mixture containing 400 mL of 0.1 M H2SO4 and 400 mL of 0.1 M NaOH will be approximately 1.3.
(b) Ionic product of water is temperature dependent.
(c) A monobasic acid with Ka=10-5 has a pH=5 . The degree of dissociation of this acid is 50% .
(d) The Le Chatelier's principle is not applicable to common-ion effect.
The correct statements are:
question:
Consider the following statements
(a) The pH of a mixture containing 400 mL of 0.1 M H2SO4 and 400 mL of 0.1 M NaOH will be approximately 1.3.
(b) Ionic product of water is temperature dependent.
(c) A monobasic acid with Ka=10-5 has a pH=5 . The degree of dissociation of this acid is 50% .
(d) The Le Chatelier's principle is not applicable to common-ion effect.
The correct statements are:
solution:
Strong acid + Strong base mixture
n H2SO4>n[NaOH] So H+ will be more
H+=2×0.1×400-0.1×400800=0.05
pH=-log⁡H+=-log⁡0.05=1.3
(b) The ionic product of water is temperature dependent
(c) HCOOH⇌H++HCOO- (weak acid)
ka=Cα21-α
ka=Cα.α1-α
ka=H+α1-α
10-5=10-5×α1-α
1-α=α
α=12=0.5
(d) Le-Chatelier’s Principle in applicable on Common Ion Effect

q: The minimum volume of water required to dissolve 0.1 g lead (II) chloride to get a saturated solution ( <math><msub><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>s</mi><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub></math> of <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi><mi>b</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3.2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup></math> ;atomic mass of <math><mi>P</mi><mi>b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>207</mn><mi> </mi><mi>u</mi></math> ) is:
question: The minimum volume of water required to dissolve 0.1 g lead (II) chloride to get a saturated solution ( <math><msub><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>s</mi><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub></math> of <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi><mi>b</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3.2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup></math> ;atomic mass of <math><mi>P</mi><mi>b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>207</mn><mi> </mi><mi>u</mi></math> ) is:
solution:
(Ksp)PbCl2=32×10-9

PbCl2=Pb2+  s   +2Cl- 2s

Ksp= Pb2+ 2Cl-2

Ksp=4s3 = 32×10-9

s3 = 8×10-9

s=2×10-3M

wM.w×1VL=2×10-3

0.1278×1VL=2×10-3

VL=0.1×1000278×2=0.18 L

subject: Chemistry
q: How do we differentiate between $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ in group III?
question: How do we differentiate between $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ in group III?
solution: $\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}$ions are increased to suppress release of $\mathrm{OH}$ ions, hence solubility product of $\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_3$ is attained. Colour of precipitate is different.

subject: Chemistry
q: pH of water is 7 at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If water is heated to $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$., it's pH will :
question: pH of water is 7 at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If water is heated to $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$., it's pH will :
solution:
At $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, pure water has $\mathrm{pH}=7$
As temperature increased, water molecules dissociate more into hydrogen ions ( $\mathrm{H}^{+}$) and hydroxide ions $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$.
This increased dissociation leads to slightly decrease in pH .
At $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{pH} \approx 6.93$

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is a Lewis acid?
question: Which of the following is a Lewis acid?
solution: As boron has vacant p-orbital (electron-deficient species), it will behave like a Lewis acid.

subject: Chemistry
q: What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of a $10^{-4} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{OH}^{-}$solution at $330 \mathrm{~K}$, if $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}$ at $330 \mathrm{~K}$ is $10^{-13.6}$ ?
question: What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of a $10^{-4} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{OH}^{-}$solution at $330 \mathrm{~K}$, if $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}$ at $330 \mathrm{~K}$ is $10^{-13.6}$ ?
solution:
Given at $330 \mathrm{~K}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{K}_w & =10^{-13.6} \\
\text { i.e. } \quad \mathrm{pK}_w & =\mathrm{pH}+\mathrm{pOH} \\
\because \quad \mathrm{pOH}^2 & =-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] \\
13.6 & =\mathrm{pH}+\mathrm{pOH} \\
\mathrm{pOH} & =-\log 10^{-4} \\
13.6 & =\mathrm{pH}+4 \\
\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH} & =13.6-4 \\
& =9.6
\end{aligned}
$$
